papers.title: Deep Learning-driven synergistic engineering of PET hydrolase for post-consumer PET depolymerization.
papers.abstract: Enzymatic depolymerization of polyethylene terephthalate (PET) offers a promising route to mitigate the increasingly severe problem of plastic pollution. However, the development of highly efficient PET hydrolases capable of processing post-consumer PET remains a critical challenge. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) provide new opportunities to accelerate the enzyme engineering of PET hydrolases. Here, we report a systematic computational redesign of the PET hydrolase NI (ThcCut1-AICCG-H185N/F189I) using the deep-learning framework, EITLEM-Kinetics. By integrating mutation free-e
papers.summary: Researchers used a deep learning framework called EITLEM-Kinetics to computationally redesign a PET hydrolase enzyme, resulting in a variant with enhanced activity for breaking down post-consumer PET plastic. This AI-driven approach successfully engineered a more efficient enzyme for plastic depolymerization, offering a promising solution to plastic pollution.

papers.title: Exploration and experimental validation of oxidative stress-related diagnostic genes in interstitial cystitis based on transcriptomics.
papers.abstract: Interstitial cystitis (IC) is a chronic pain syndrome with an elusive diagnosis and poorly understood pathogenesis, in which oxidative stress (OS) is increasingly implicated. This study aimed to identify and validate OS-related diagnostic biomarkers for IC using an integrative computational and experimental approach.
papers.summary: This study identifies and validates oxidative stress-related genes as potential diagnostic biomarkers for interstitial cystitis by combining computational analysis of transcriptomic data with experimental confirmation. The findings suggest that specific oxidative stress genes could help improve diagnosis and understanding of this chronic pain condition.

papers.title: Monodisperse PEG engineering for quantifiable surface conjugation on PLGA nanoparticles.
papers.abstract: Surface PEGylation is a well-established strategy to enhance the stability, functionality, and bioavailability of nanoparticles (NPs) in biomedical applications. However, the widespread use of polydisperse PEG derivatives limits the ability to precisely quantify surface PEGylation efficiency (mol%) and evaluate its effect on NP properties. In this study, we present a systematic approach to engineer PLGA NPs using structurally defined monodisperse PEG-diamine derivatives of varying chain lengths (PEG6, PEG26, PEG45), synthesized and purified by chromatographic methods and covalently attached vi
papers.summary: This paper presents a method for engineering PLGA nanoparticles with precisely defined monodisperse PEG chains, enabling accurate quantification of surface PEGylation efficiency. The approach overcomes limitations of traditional polydisperse PEG derivatives, allowing better control over nanoparticle properties for biomedical applications.

papers.title: Enterovirus RNase L inhibiting RNAs are highly conserved with limited phylogenetic distribution.
papers.abstract: RNA molecules form specific 3D structures that facilitate a variety of functions through interactions with other macromolecules. Many RNA viral genomes maintain these structures to interact with and evade host immunity machinery. One such element, the competitive inhibitor RNA (ciRNA), discovered in the protein coding region of the poliovirus serotype 1 (PV1) genome, inhibits a host antiviral protein, ribonuclease L (RNase L). Although some functionally essential structural motifs of the PV1 ciRNA have been studied, the extent of its evolutionary conservation and other structural requirements
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study investigates the evolutionary conservation of competitive inhibitor RNAs (ciRNAs) that allow enteroviruses to evade host antiviral defenses by inhibiting RNase L. The researchers found that these ciRNA elements are highly conserved across enteroviruses but have a limited phylogenetic distribution, suggesting they evolved in specific viral lineages. The findings provide insight into how RNA structures in viral genomes can be maintained to counteract host immunity.

papers.title: Pep2Mol: 3D Molecule Generation Targeting Protein-Protein Interfaces with Diffusion Models.
papers.abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are central to biological processes. Designing small molecules that modulate dysregulated PPIs holds strong promise for targeting undruggable proteins. However, existing structure-based drug design approaches focus on well-defined small-molecule binding pockets and struggle to generalize to large, shallow, and chemically complex PPI interfaces. Here, we introduce Pep2Mol, a diffusion-based generative model for 3D molecule design that targets orthosteric PPI sites by explicitly incorporating binding peptides or proteins as structural guidance, moving beyond c
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: Pep2Mol is a new AI model that uses diffusion to generate 3D small molecules specifically designed to target protein-protein interaction (PPI) interfaces, which are often difficult to drug. By incorporating the structure of binding peptides as guidance, it overcomes limitations of existing methods that struggle with the shallow, complex surfaces of PPI sites. This approach could enable drug discovery for previously 'undruggable' proteins involved in many diseases.

papers.title: Thioredoxin Reductase as a Target for Antibacterial Gold Compounds in Burkholderia cenocepacia: Disclosing the Molecular Basis of Enzyme Inhibition.
papers.abstract: Thioredoxin reductase from Burkholderia cenocepacia (Bc-TrxR) is a recognized intracellular target of antibacterial gold(I) compounds, yet the molecular basis of enzyme inhibition remains unclear. Here, we report an integrated structural, biophysical, and biochemical investigation of recombinant Bc-TrxR and its interaction with three prototypical gold(I) agents: auranofin (AF) and two trimethylphosphine-thiolate derivatives (Au1 and Au2), previously identified as potent enzyme inhibitors. The enzyme was expressed, purified, and its crystal structure solved at 2.52 Å resolution, revealing a hom
papers.journal: Chembiochem : a European journal of chemical biology
papers.summary: This study reveals how gold-based antibacterial compounds inhibit thioredoxin reductase in Burkholderia cenocepacia, a pathogen causing lung infections. By solving the enzyme's crystal structure and analyzing its interactions with three gold compounds, the researchers provide the first detailed molecular understanding of this inhibition mechanism. This work could guide development of new antibiotics against drug-resistant bacteria.

papers.title: Insights into the toxic effects of moxifloxacin in Allium cepa through multiparametric experimental and in silico analyses.
papers.abstract: In this study, the cytogenetic and biochemical effects of moxifloxacin (MOX), a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic, on Allium cepa L., a non-target plant model, were investigated using a combination of experimental and in silico approaches. Onion roots were exposed to MOX at concentrations of 15, 29, and 57 µg/L. Genotoxicity was assessed using the mitotic index (MI), micronucleus (MN), chromosomal aberrations (CAs), and the Comet assay. The findings showed that MOX reduced mitotic activity in a dose-dependent manner (by 21% in the highest dose group) and significantly increased the
papers.summary: This study shows that the antibiotic moxifloxacin (MOX) causes genotoxic and biochemical damage in onion plants (Allium cepa), a non-target organism. Exposure to MOX reduced cell division and increased DNA damage in a dose-dependent manner, raising concerns about its environmental impact.

papers.title: Cryo-EM structure of the complex between transglutaminase 2 and the 45 kDa domain of fibronectin.
papers.abstract: Fibronectin (FN) and transglutaminase 2 (TG2) engage in a high-affinity interaction. This interaction affects anchoring, migration and survival of cells, and high cellular expression of TG2 is associated with metastasis potential of cancers. Previous work has mapped the interaction to involve the N-terminal part of TG2 and an N-terminally located 45 kDa domain of FN (FN45). In particular, the I8 module of FN45 is reported to be necessary and sufficient for TG2 binding. Here, we present the cryo-EM structure of a complex between human TG2 and FN45 at 2.8 Å resolution. In the structure, the dist
papers.summary: This study presents the first high-resolution cryo-EM structure of the complex between transglutaminase 2 (TG2) and the 45 kDa domain of fibronectin (FN45), revealing the molecular details of their high-affinity interaction. The structure shows how the I8 module of FN45 binds to TG2, providing insights into how this interaction influences cell adhesion, migration, and cancer metastasis.

papers.title: Rational engineering of the Arabidopsis thaliana plant cysteine oxidase 4 active site can reduce biochemical activity and improve submergence tolerance.
papers.abstract: The increase in flooding events due to climate change is having devastating effects on food security. Plants can respond to submergence by reducing their energy requirements and switching their metabolism from oxidative phosphorylation to fermentation. This response is promoted by group VII ethylene response factors (ERF-VIIs) whose stability is dependent on oxygen availability; in normoxia, plant cysteine oxidase (PCO) enzymes catalyze oxidation of ERF-VII N-terminal Cys residues to trigger ERF-VII degradation via the Cys/Arg-N-degron pathway. In hypoxia, reduced PCO activity prevents ERF-VII
papers.summary: This study shows that engineering the active site of the Arabidopsis cysteine oxidase PCO4 can reduce its biochemical activity, which in turn improves submergence tolerance in plants. By slowing the oxygen-dependent degradation of group VII ethylene response factors (ERF-VIIs), the engineered enzyme helps plants better survive flooding conditions.

papers.title: Profiling fungal secondary metabolites with anti-leishmanial activity: an integrated in silico and in vitro study.
papers.abstract: Numerous reports of drug resistance in Leishmania major, the causative agent of cutaneous leishmaniasis (CL), underscore the need to discover novel therapeutics. Fungal secondary metabolites (SMs) are valuable resources for drug development and can serve as antimicrobial agents, enzyme inhibitors, and lead compounds for novel medicines. A three-dimensional model of the L. major sterol 14α-demethylase (LmCYP51) enzyme was built using L. infantum CYP51 as a template. A library of 1,167 compounds was virtually screened against LmCYP51 using high-throughput docking. Eleven promising candidates wer
papers.summary: This study used computational modeling and high-throughput docking to screen over 1,100 fungal secondary metabolites against a key enzyme (LmCYP51) from Leishmania major, identifying 11 promising candidates for anti-leishmanial drug development. The findings highlight fungal natural products as a potential source of new therapeutics for cutaneous leishmaniasis, which faces growing drug resistance.

papers.title: Mechanistic and structural elucidation of π-π end-stacking interactions of a novel SO2NH-anthraquinone derivative with telomeric G-quadruplex d-[TTAGGGT]4: integrated NMR, spectroscopic, electrochemical, and computational evidence.
papers.abstract: Targeting telomeric G-quadruplex (G4) DNA has emerged as a promising anticancer strategy by inhibiting telomerase activity and disrupting telomere maintenance. In the present study, we report a comprehensive structural, spectroscopic, electrochemical, and computational investigation of a novel anthracene-9,10-dione derivative, N-(9,10-dioxo-9,10-dihydroanthracen-1-yl)-3-methyl-2-(phenylsulfonamido)butanamide (1-VAQ), and its interaction with the parallel G-quadruplex d-[TTAGGGT]4. To evaluate binding selectivity, comparative studies were also performed using duplex DNA as a control. 1H NMR spe
papers.summary: This study investigates how a novel anthraquinone derivative binds to telomeric G-quadruplex DNA, a potential anticancer target. Using multiple techniques including NMR and computational modeling, the researchers found that the compound interacts through π-π end-stacking with the G-quadruplex structure, showing selectivity over duplex DNA. This work provides mechanistic insights that could aid in designing more effective G-quadruplex-targeting anticancer drugs.

papers.title: In silico discovery and interaction analysis of a Datura stramonium defensin-like peptide targeting cancer-related receptors.
papers.abstract: Cancer continues to be a major global health burden, with receptor tyrosine kinases such as EGFR, ERBB2, and VEGFR-3 being critical therapeutic targets due to their central roles in tumor growth, survival, and angiogenesis. Current therapies, while effective in some contexts, face limitations including resistance, toxicity, and high cost, highlighting the need for novel multi-target approaches. In this study, we report the isolation and computational characterization of a novel defensin-like peptide (DEFL) from Datura stramonium (GenBank accession KT371458). The peptide sequence encoded 74 ami
papers.summary: Researchers computationally identified a new defensin-like peptide from the Datura stramonium plant and analyzed its potential to bind multiple cancer-related receptors (EGFR, ERBB2, VEGFR-3), suggesting it could serve as a multi-target therapeutic candidate. The study combines sequence isolation with in silico interaction analysis to propose a novel peptide-based approach for overcoming limitations of current cancer therapies.

papers.title: Designing a chimeric multi-epitope vaccine against Candida auris using reverse vaccinology approach targeting the agglutinin-like protein N-terminal domain.
papers.abstract: Candida auris is an emerging multidrug resistant fungal pathogen associated with high mortality rates, rapid global dissemination and resistance to conventional antifungal therapies. It's remarkable ability to evade host immune responses and persist in health care setting demands the development of effective immunotherapeutic strategies. In this study, a reverse vaccinology and immunoinformatics based approach was employed to design a novel chimeric multi-epitope vaccine targeting surface expose N-terminal domain of the agglutinin like protein involved in host pathogen interactions. High affin
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to design a multi-epitope vaccine against the drug-resistant fungus Candida auris by targeting a key surface protein. The vaccine candidate shows promise in computer simulations for triggering immune responses, but requires experimental validation.

papers.title: Novel imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-based α-glucosidase inhibitors: synthesis, biological evaluations, and computational studies.
papers.abstract: In an attempt to identify novel α‑glucosidase inhibitors, a new series of substituted imidazo[1,2‑a]pyridine derivatives (10a-10ab) was designed, synthesized, and evaluated for their inhibitory activities. All compounds exhibited potent inhibition, with IC50 values ranging from 12.01 to 93.01 µM, significantly better than acarbose IC50 = 750.02 µM). SAR analysis highlighted the critical role of para‑substitution on the benzamide ring, especially methoxy groups. The most potent compound, 10s (IC50 = 12.01 µM), acted as a competitive inhibitor (Ki = 21 µM) with no α‑amylase inhibition, indicatin
papers.summary: Researchers designed and synthesized a new series of imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine derivatives that are potent α-glucosidase inhibitors, with the best compound (10s) showing 62-fold better activity than the standard drug acarbose. The compound acts as a competitive inhibitor and does not inhibit α-amylase, suggesting potential for diabetes treatment with reduced side effects.

papers.title: Melatonin reprograms antioxidant defenses to suppress ferroptosis via Homer1a/mGluR1-Nrf2/xCT signaling after retinal ischemia-reperfusion.
papers.abstract: Melatonin (Mel) exerts antioxidant and anti-ferroptosis effects not only via the canonical receptors Mt1/Mt2 but also via metabotropic glutamate receptor 1 (mGluR1)/glutamate-mediated signaling. Nevertheless, the regulatory mechanism by which Mel modulates mGluR1/glutamate signaling in retinal ischemia-reperfusion (I/R) injury and its downstream effects on ferroptosis and oxidative stress remain poorly understood. Here, bulk RNA sequencing identified that the Mel receptors Mt1/Mt2 and ferroptosis/iron-redox regulators (Gpx4, Fth1, and xCT) were significantly downregulated after retinal I/R. Me
papers.summary: This study reveals that melatonin protects against retinal ischemia-reperfusion injury by activating the Homer1a/mGluR1-Nrf2/xCT signaling pathway, which reprograms antioxidant defenses and suppresses ferroptosis. The findings identify a non-canonical mechanism of melatonin action beyond its known receptors, offering potential therapeutic targets for retinal diseases involving oxidative stress and iron-dependent cell death.

papers.title: Target deconvolution of a selective HepG2 cytotoxic hit identifies GSTP1 and TRAP1 as candidate molecular targets.
papers.abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) remains a lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options in advanced disease, highlighting the need to identify selective cytotoxic agents and biologically relevant molecular targets for early anticancer drug discovery. In this study, a focused in-house compound set was screened against HepG2, MCF-7, and MDA-MB-231 cancer cell lines using the MTT assay to identify compounds with selective activity toward HCC-derived cells. The most active hit, HTS00019, was then subjected to an integrated computational target-deconvolution workflow comprising SEA target predi
papers.summary: This study identified a compound (HTS00019) that selectively kills liver cancer cells (HepG2) and used computational methods to predict its molecular targets, revealing GSTP1 and TRAP1 as likely candidates. This work provides a foundation for developing targeted therapies against hepatocellular carcinoma.

papers.title: Association between serum perfluoroalkyl substances and COPD risk: population evidence and in vitro validation.
papers.abstract: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances are characterized by environmental persistence, bioaccumulation potential, and multi-organ toxicity. Given their ubiquitous presence in the environment and human serum, concerns regarding respiratory health risks are growing, particularly due to scarce evidence on environmental etiologies of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease in never-smokers. This study aimed to investigate the mechanistic role of these substances in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease through a population-computational-experimental paradigm, with specific focus on lipid-metabolic medi
papers.summary: This study investigates the link between perfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in blood and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) risk, using population data, computational analysis, and lab experiments. The findings suggest PFAS exposure may contribute to COPD, potentially through lipid metabolism disruption, providing new evidence for environmental causes of COPD in non-smokers.

papers.title: Design, synthesis, biological assessment, and integrated computational analysis of new pyrazole-based antimicrobial candidates.
papers.abstract: The emergence of antimicrobial resistance needs the development of new chemotherapeutic scaffolds. Pyrazole derivatives are recognized for their broad biological activity; however, further structural innovation is required to enhance antimicrobial efficacy and safety. Thus, new pyrazole-based candidates were designed and synthesized using 3-(4-chlorophenyl)-1-phenylpyrazol-4-yl-2-cyanoacryloyl chloride as a versatile precursor. A series of mono- and bidentate nucleophile-derived compounds was prepared and evaluated for antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria and
papers.summary: This study reports the design and synthesis of new pyrazole-based compounds as potential antimicrobial agents to combat drug resistance. The compounds were tested against bacteria, showing promising activity, and computational analysis supported their mechanism of action. This work provides a new chemical scaffold for further antimicrobial development.

papers.title: Design, fabrication and molecular dynamics simulations of luteolin pickering emulsion using phosphorylated and succinylated lactoferrin nanocomplex.
papers.abstract: Chemically modified protein-based nanocomplexes are widely used as food-grade carriers, but their molecular mechanisms remain unclear. This study developed Pickering emulsions (PEs) composed of phosphorylated (PLF) or succinylated (SLF) lactoferrin (LF) complexed with luteolin (LUT) via a pH-driven method, integrating experimental and computational approaches. SLF-LUT PEs showed superior physicochemical properties, including smaller droplet sizes, encapsulation efficiency up to 80% under neutral conditions, and enhanced storage stability at pH 8 over 30 days. SLF-LUT also significantly inhibit
papers.summary: This study developed Pickering emulsions using chemically modified lactoferrin (phosphorylated or succinylated) complexed with luteolin, finding that succinylated lactoferrin produced smaller droplets, higher encapsulation efficiency (up to 80%), and better storage stability. Molecular dynamics simulations helped explain the improved performance of the succinylated formulation. The work provides a food-grade delivery system for bioactive compounds with enhanced stability.

papers.title: Perceptual interactions and predicted mechanism in binary odorant mixtures of baked green tea (Camellia sinensis cv. Zhongcha 302).
papers.abstract: Tea is globally prized for its flavor, with aroma being a key quality indicator. To fully understand tea aroma, which arises from complex perceptual interactions, systematic molecular sensory omics that integrate both perceptual complexity and matrix effects are required. This critical integration continues to be unmet as a key challenge in the field.
papers.summary: This study investigates how binary mixtures of odorants from baked green tea interact perceptually, using molecular sensory omics to model these interactions. The authors identify key odorants and predict the mechanisms behind their perceptual blending, addressing a gap in understanding complex tea aroma.

papers.title: Proteolysis-driven enhancement of texture and bioactivity in milk co-fermented with Lactobacillus delbrueckii ssp. bulgaricus, Lacticaseibacillus paracasei, and Kluyveromyces marxianus.
papers.abstract: This work aims to elucidate how the inclusion of Kluyveromyces marxianus DPUL-F15 combined with Lactobacillus delbrueckii ssp. bulgaricus DPUL-F36 and Lacticaseibacillus paracasei DPUL-F115 enhances the textural and functional characteristics of fermented milk through accelerated proteolysis. The results revealed that fermented milk inoculated solely with Lactobacillus delbrueckii ssp. bulgaricus DPUL-F36 and Lacticaseibacillus paracasei DPUL-F115 served as the yeast-free control, while the 3-strain co-fermented milk exhibited significantly stronger acidification and proteolytic activities dur
papers.summary: This study shows that adding the yeast Kluyveromyces marxianus to a standard bacterial starter culture accelerates protein breakdown in fermented milk, resulting in improved texture and increased bioactive peptide content. The three-strain co-fermentation produced milk with stronger acidification and proteolytic activity compared to the bacterial-only control.

papers.title: Subtype-selective binding of milk β-casomorphins across the opioid receptor family revealed by molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulation.
papers.abstract: β-casomorphins (BCMs) are opioid-like peptides produced during the digestion of β-casein in the gastrointestinal tract. They are also involved in other gastrointestinal and neurophysiological processes. Although the effect of BCM7 on the μ-opioid receptor (MOR) has been extensively studied, the molecular interaction mechanisms of other BCMs with different opioid receptors have not been elucidated. This study aimed to investigate the receptor-specific binding behavior of BCM3-BCM11 across the μ-, δ-, κ-, and nociceptin/orphanin FQ peptide (NOP) opioid receptors using structure-based computation
papers.summary: This study uses computational modeling to investigate how different milk-derived opioid peptides (β-casomorphins) bind to various opioid receptors, revealing that different peptide lengths have distinct receptor preferences. The findings provide molecular-level insights into how these dietary peptides might selectively activate different opioid receptor subtypes, which could explain their varied physiological effects.

papers.title: Mechano-reprogramming of tumor-associated macrophages by engineering nanogel stiffness for suppression of tumor development.
papers.abstract: Nanoparticle mechanical properties, as critical factors in tumor targeting drug delivery, have recently been revealed to regulate cellular biological functions. While macroscopic mechanical stimuli are demonstrated to reprogram tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), it is unclear how TAMs respond to microscopic stimuli from nanoparticles. Herein, we demonstrated that the stiff 15%NGs presented stronger interactions with integrin than the soft 2%NGs, promoting the polymerization of actin and the activation of yes-associated protein 1. Therefore, the pro-inflammatory phenotype of TAMs was enhanced
papers.summary: This study shows that the stiffness of nanogels used for drug delivery can reprogram tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) from a pro-tumor to a pro-inflammatory state. Stiffer nanogels interact more strongly with integrin on TAMs, activating actin polymerization and YAP1 signaling to enhance anti-tumor immune responses. This mechano-reprogramming strategy offers a new approach to suppress tumor development by engineering nanoparticle mechanical properties.

papers.title: Design and Engineering of an Artificial Bifunctional N-Deacetylase/N-Sulfotransferase for the Biosynthesis of N-Sulfated Heparosan.
papers.abstract: Heparin is a widely used anticoagulant, while its traditional animal-derived production faces significant challenges in safety and scalability. The active expression of the initiating-step bifunctional enzyme N-deacetylase/N-sulfotransferase (NDST) in bacteria remains a key bottleneck in heparin biosynthesis. Here, we firstly mined and characterized diverse polysaccharide N-deacetylases (NDases) in Escherichia coli and revealed several NDases that are active toward the deacetylation of heparosan. An artificial NDDr-RL-ST was designed and constructed by fusing an active NDase with a heterogenou
papers.summary: This study addresses a key bottleneck in heparin biosynthesis by engineering an artificial bifunctional enzyme that can perform both deacetylation and sulfation steps in bacteria. The researchers identified active bacterial deacetylases and fused them with a sulfotransferase to create a functional enzyme for producing N-sulfated heparosan, a heparin precursor. This work could enable more scalable and safer production of heparin compared to traditional animal-derived methods.

papers.title: Host-dependent optimization of rabies virus-like particle making in insect cells using a low-level control system.
papers.abstract: Rabies causes over 59,000 deaths annually, highlighting the need for scalable and cost-effective vaccines. Virus-like particles (VLP) are promising candidates, but their production depends on host physiology and process parameters. Here, rabies VLP were produced in Spodoptera frugiperda (Sf9) and High Five insect cells, employing a three-level full factorial design to optimize the impact of the expression system, time of infection (TOI), and harvest time (HT). The cell lines exhibited complementary expression profiles: Sf9 achieved the highest rabies virus glycoprotein (RVGP) expression, reach
papers.summary: This study optimized the production of rabies virus-like particles (VLPs) in two insect cell lines (Sf9 and High Five) using a three-level factorial design to improve vaccine scalability. The key finding is that Sf9 cells achieved the highest rabies glycoprotein expression, while the two cell lines showed complementary expression profiles depending on infection and harvest timing. This work provides a systematic approach to enhance VLP yield for cost-effective rabies vaccine development.

papers.title: Aromatic π-stacking stabilizes an α-helical SARS-CoV-2 MPER peptide that mimics the post-fusion spike and enables potent antiviral activity.
papers.abstract: Membrane fusion between SARS-CoV-2 and host cells is mediated by the spike protein and involves the membrane-proximal external region (MPER), a tryptophan-rich sequence implicated in viral entry. Here, we investigated the feasibility of MPER-derived peptides as potential antiviral agents and examined the structural determinants underlying their activity. A series of truncated MPER peptides was synthesized and evaluated for antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2 in cellular assays, revealing potent inhibitory potency against different variants, with nanomolar IC50 values associated with sequence
papers.summary: This study shows that short peptides derived from the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein's membrane-proximal external region (MPER) can potently inhibit viral entry, with aromatic π-stacking interactions stabilizing an α-helical structure that mimics the post-fusion spike. The most active peptides achieved nanomolar IC50 values against multiple SARS-CoV-2 variants, suggesting a promising new class of antiviral agents.

papers.title: Theoretical study on N(sp2)+-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds in the context of drug discovery.
papers.abstract: C-H···O hydrogen bonds are regarded as weak and overlooked in structure-based drug design. Here, we demonstrate through quantum-chemical calculations that N(sp2)⁺-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds involving pyridinium ions exhibit strong and directional interactions with carbonyl oxygen atoms. Density-functional-theory (DFT) and natural-bond-orbital (NBO) analyses indicated that cationization increases the positive charge on adjacent sp2 C-H protons, thereby enhancing their proton-donating ability. Fragment-based symmetry-adapted-perturbation-theory (F-SAPT) analyses revealed that these interactions
papers.summary: This study uses quantum-chemical calculations to show that N(sp2)+-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds involving pyridinium ions are stronger and more directional than typical C-H···O hydrogen bonds, due to increased positive charge on the C-H protons. The findings suggest these interactions could be valuable for structure-based drug design, where they are often overlooked.

papers.title: A structure-based epitope tagging approach identifies vulnerable sites on the malarial P36-P52 protein complex for antibody-mediated neutralization of Plasmodium sporozoites.
papers.abstract: Malaria is caused by apicomplexan parasites of the genus Plasmodium, which are transmitted through the bite of Anopheles mosquitoes that inject sporozoites (SPZs) into the skin. SPZs migrate to and infect the liver for an initial round of replication. SPZs and liver stages have long been considered as ideal targets for malaria vaccines. The main SPZ surface protein, the circumsporozoite protein (CSP), is the target of currently approved malaria vaccines and prophylactic antibody therapies. Studies in rodent malaria models have shown that anti-CSP antibodies exert their protective effect mainly
papers.summary: This study uses a structure-based epitope tagging approach to identify vulnerable sites on the P36-P52 protein complex of Plasmodium sporozoites, which are targeted by antibodies to neutralize the parasite. The findings highlight potential new targets for malaria vaccine development beyond the currently targeted circumsporozoite protein.

papers.title: Structure of the Beat-Side complex reveals their interaction is required for neuromuscular system wiring.
papers.abstract: Nervous system assembly is guided by the actions of cell surface receptors. In Drosophila, members of the beaten path (Beat) and sidestep (Side) protein families have been described as axon guidance receptor-cue pairs, in addition to roles in specifying synaptic connectivity in the optic lobe. To understand the molecular basis and specificity of Beat-Side interactions, we report here the structure of a Beat-Side complex, Beat-Vc bound to Side-VI. The structure shows a binding topology similar to other immunoglobulin superfamily receptors, especially PD-1-PD-L1, nectins, SynCAMs, Dprs, and DIPs
papers.summary: This study reports the first structure of a Beat-Side protein complex (Beat-Vc bound to Side-VI) from Drosophila, revealing a binding topology similar to other immunoglobulin superfamily receptors like PD-1-PD-L1. The findings demonstrate that Beat-Side interactions are essential for proper neuromuscular system wiring, providing molecular insights into axon guidance and synaptic connectivity.

papers.title: Selection of Novel DNA Aptamers against HPV16 Biomarker, E6: An In Silico Approach.
papers.abstract: HPV16 E6 oncoprotein is a critical driver of cervical cancer, necessitating high-specificity diagnostic and therapeutic agents. This study presents an in silico selection of DNA aptamers targeting the HPV16 E6 oncoprotein through a pipeline of structure-based virtual screening and 250 ns Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations, performed with GROMACS 2019 and the CHARMM36M. Using AutoDock VinaXB and HADDOCK, eight candidates were evaluated against the F2 RNA benchmark. MM/PBSA calculations identified Apt 9 (-143.76 kcal/mol) as the most potent lead, surpassing the affinity of the F2 control (-142.
papers.summary: This study used computer simulations to identify DNA aptamers that bind to the HPV16 E6 oncoprotein, a key driver of cervical cancer. Through virtual screening and molecular dynamics simulations, one aptamer (Apt 9) showed stronger predicted binding than an existing benchmark, suggesting it could serve as a new diagnostic or therapeutic agent.

papers.title: A comprehensive dataset of 32 million pentapeptide structures for high-throughput virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Small peptides are widely used as binders, modulators, and structural motifs, but their conformational flexibility complicates structure-based analysis and high-throughput screening. We present an open dataset of three-dimensional structures for the complete space of canonical amino-acid pentapeptides: 3,200,000 unique sequences with up to 10 conformers per sequence, for a total of 32,000,000 peptide conformers. Structures were generated directly from sequence using an automated workflow built on UCSF ChimeraX for model construction, Reduce for hydrogen placement, and RDKit for conformer gener
papers.summary: Researchers created an open dataset of 32 million 3D structures covering all possible pentapeptide sequences made from standard amino acids. This resource enables high-throughput virtual screening of small peptides for use as binders, modulators, or structural motifs without requiring experimental structure determination.

papers.title: Targeting breast cancer with 1,2,4-trioxanes: synthesis, cytotoxicity, and molecular docking insights.
papers.abstract: Breast cancer remains the most common malignancy among women to date, with increasing incidence and resistance to conventional therapies driving the search for novel treatments. 1,2,4-Trioxanes, known for their antimalarial activity, have emerged as promising anticancer agents due to their ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) through iron-mediated activation, selectively inducing apoptosis in cancer cells. In this study, a series of hydroxy-functionalized and hemi-succinate trioxane derivatives were synthesized and evaluated against the MCF-7 breast cancer cell line. Among them, c
papers.summary: This study synthesized and tested new 1,2,4-trioxane derivatives against MCF-7 breast cancer cells, finding that some compounds showed promising cytotoxicity. The compounds work by generating reactive oxygen species through iron-mediated activation, selectively killing cancer cells. Molecular docking provided insights into their potential mechanism of action.

papers.title: Unveiling acetamide coupled benzothiazole hydrazones as potential anti-proliferative agents: an experimental and computational approach.
papers.abstract: A series of benzothiazole acetamide hydrazones (8a-p) was synthesized and evaluated for anti-proliferative activity against various human cancer cell lines, including a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CAPAN-1), a colorectal carcinoma (HCT-116), a leukemia (HL60), a glioblastoma (LN229), a T lymphoblast (Molt-4), a non-small cell lung cancer (NCI-H460), and a B lymphoblast (Z138) cell line. Among the tested derivatives, compounds 8l, 8n, and 8o emerged as lead compounds, exhibiting cytotoxicity across multiple cell lines. Notably, compound 8o demonstrated selective cytotoxicity towards Z138 a
papers.summary: This study synthesized and tested a series of benzothiazole acetamide hydrazones against seven human cancer cell lines, identifying three lead compounds (8l, 8n, 8o) with broad anti-proliferative activity. One compound (8o) showed selective cytotoxicity against a B lymphoblast cell line, suggesting potential for targeted cancer therapy.

papers.title: Pharmacophore-based virtual screening, molecular docking, MD simulation, MM-GBSA energy calculation and DFT analysis for the in silico discovery of a SaFtsZ inhibitor.
papers.abstract: The rapid emergence of antimicrobial resistance demands the discovery of new antibacterial targets and inhibitors. Staphylococcus aureus filamenting temperature-sensitive protein (SaFtsZ), an essential cytoskeletal protein involved in bacterial cytokinesis and Z-ring formation, has gained attention as a promising target for antibacterial drug discovery. In the present study, an integrated computational strategy involving pharmacophore mapping, molecular docking, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, and density functional theory (DFT) analysis was employed to identify potential SaFtsZ inhibitor
papers.summary: This study uses a computational pipeline to identify potential inhibitors of SaFtsZ, a bacterial protein essential for cell division, as a new approach to combat antimicrobial resistance. The authors combined pharmacophore modeling, molecular docking, dynamics simulations, and quantum chemical calculations to screen for candidate compounds. The work provides a set of computationally predicted inhibitors that could be experimentally validated for antibacterial activity.

papers.title: ScrambleBench: a workflow for comparative assessment of structure-based de novo generative models.
papers.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) has rapidly advanced over the past decade in the field of drug discovery, particularly for the de novo design of small molecules based on target protein structures. While generative AI has the potential to complement traditional structure-based drug design (SBDD) to discover novel hits, their performance is often assessed using non-standardized evaluation criteria. As the number of generative AI models continue to grow, it becomes increasingly important to determine whether these tools are sufficiently robust and reliable for integration into medicinal c
papers.summary: This paper introduces ScrambleBench, a standardized workflow for comparing structure-based generative AI models used in drug discovery. The tool addresses the lack of consistent evaluation criteria, helping researchers determine if these models are robust enough for practical medicinal chemistry use.

papers.title: Multiply Perturbed Response: A Computational Protocol to Identify Cooperative Allosteric Residue Combinations Driving Protein Conformational Transitions.
papers.abstract: Protein function often depends on dynamic conformational transitions driven by external factors or molecular interactions. Understanding the allosteric mechanisms underlying these transitions is essential for mechanistic insight into protein function. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are widely used to study protein dynamics; however, capturing large-scale, rare transitions is computationally expensive. To address this, we previously developed Perturbation Response Scanning (PRS), based on elastic network models and linear response theory, but PRS is limited in capturing collective effects
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational protocol called Multiply Perturbed Response (MPR) that extends the Perturbation Response Scanning method to identify cooperative combinations of allosteric residues driving protein conformational changes. MPR uses elastic network models and linear response theory to efficiently predict how multiple residue perturbations collectively influence protein dynamics, overcoming limitations of single-residue approaches. The protocol provides a practical tool for studying allosteric mechanisms and could aid in drug discovery and protein engineering.

papers.title: Eras of bioinformatics technologies from command-line interfaces to artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots.
papers.abstract: Over the past 75 years, especially the recent quarter century, bioinformatics has undergone a profound transformation, evolving from a specialized field of command-line tools to a cornerstone of modern biomedical and life sciences. We trace this journey through distinct technological eras driven by exponential biological data growth and parallel computational advances. The genomic revolution established foundational sequence analysis tools and was rapidly followed by the next-generation sequencing era, when unprecedented data volumes shifted the bottleneck from generation to analysis. This dro
papers.summary: This review traces the historical evolution of bioinformatics over 75 years, from early command-line tools to modern AI chatbots, highlighting how exponential growth in biological data and computational advances have driven distinct technological eras. The authors focus on how the genomic revolution and next-generation sequencing shifted the bottleneck from data generation to analysis, setting the stage for AI integration.

papers.title: Diamine ligands enhance photodynamic inactivation of gram-negative bacteria using 5-aminolevulinic acid.
papers.abstract: This study investigates the enhanced efficacy of photoinduced microbial inactivation targeting both planktonic and biofilm forms of Gram-negative bacteria, specifically Acinetobacter baumannii and Proteus mirabilis. The approach utilizes diaminocyclohexane (DACH)-based diamines to augment antibacterial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) employing 5-aminolevulinic acid. Results demonstrated that blue light irradiation produced the highest bactericidal effects, achieving mortality rates of 99.9990 ± 0.0008% for P. mirabilis and 99.990 ± 0.001% for A. baumannii at a light dose of 70 J·cm-2 in the presen
papers.summary: This study shows that adding diaminocyclohexane (DACH)-based diamines to 5-aminolevulinic acid-based photodynamic therapy greatly improves killing of Gram-negative bacteria, including drug-resistant strains like Acinetobacter baumannii and Proteus mirabilis. Blue light irradiation achieved over 99.99% bacterial mortality, suggesting a promising new approach for treating biofilm-associated infections.

papers.title: Structural and biochemical insights into the antibiofilm activity of cationic lipoamino acid ester against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus.
papers.abstract: Three-dimensional structures, primarily derived from X-ray crystallography, are crucial for understanding the solid-state properties of active pharmaceuticals and for enabling structure-based drug design. Herein, we report the first single crystal X-ray structure of cationic lipoaminoacid ester [(2-((N-(2-methoxy-2-oxoethyl)tetradecanamido)methyl)-1-methylpyridin-1-ium iodide, QPyN14Ge]. QPyN14Ge crystallized in the triclinic space group P1̄. QPyN14Ge has a highly ordered and coherent packing arrangement due to tail-to-tail interdigitation of hydrophobic layers, stabilization by planar trans-a
papers.summary: This study reports the first single-crystal X-ray structure of a cationic lipoamino acid ester with antibiofilm activity against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. The compound's highly ordered packing arrangement, stabilized by hydrophobic interdigitation and planar trans conformation, provides structural insights that could inform the design of new antibiofilm agents.

papers.title: Engineering intrinsically radiolabeled nanoparticles for imaging and therapy: a decade of design strategies and insights.
papers.abstract: The convergence of nanotechnology and nuclear medicine has opened new possibilities for cancer imaging and therapy, but conventional chelator-based radiolabeling approaches often suffer from limited stability and can alter the inherent properties of nanomaterials. Intrinsic radiolabeling has emerged as a promising alternative, enabling the direct incorporation of radionuclides within the nanoparticle matrix through mechanisms such as lattice doping, isotopic substitution, coordination to structural sites, or entrapment during nanoparticle formation. In this approach, the radionuclide becomes a
papers.summary: This review summarizes a decade of progress in engineering intrinsically radiolabeled nanoparticles, which incorporate radionuclides directly into the nanoparticle matrix rather than using conventional chelator-based methods. The approach improves stability and preserves nanoparticle properties, offering promising applications for cancer imaging and therapy.

papers.title: Low-Temperature xTB-MD-DFT Screening of Functionalized Oxide Surface-Patch Models (TiO2, ZnO, CeO2) for Hydrocarbon Association and Microbial-Proxy Perturbation Assessment in Cold Bioremediation.
papers.abstract: Hydrocarbon biodegradation in cold environments is constrained not only by microbial catabolic capacity but also by interfacial access to poorly soluble substrates and by the way remediation materials interact with microbial envelope-related structures. This study presents an uncertainty-aware low-temperature computational screening workflow for prioritizing functionalized oxide surface-patch models that may favor hydrocarbon association while avoiding excessive perturbation of simplified microbial-interface proxies. Twelve finite oxide-ligand candidates derived from TiO2, ZnO, and CeO2 patche
papers.summary: This study presents a computational workflow to screen functionalized oxide surface patches (TiO2, ZnO, CeO2) for their ability to associate with hydrocarbons while minimizing disruption to microbial interfaces, aiming to improve cold bioremediation. The approach uses low-temperature simulations to prioritize materials that enhance hydrocarbon access without harming microbial structures.

papers.title: Sustained release of Eugenol from MCM-41 mesoporous silica for preservation of Agaricus bisporus.
papers.abstract: The postharvest quality of Agaricus bisporus declines rapidly during refrigerated storage. A controlled-release system (Eugenol@MCM-41) was prepared by loading eugenol into MCM-41, with a loading capacity of 51%. Physicochemical characterization and all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were combined to clarify the sustained-release mechanism. MD simulations provided supportive evidence for favorable eugenol-silica interfacial interactions and restricted surface-associated mobility in the mesoporous model. In vitro release exhibited clear medium dependence and followed the Weibull model
papers.summary: Researchers developed a controlled-release system by loading eugenol into MCM-41 mesoporous silica to preserve the quality of Agaricus bisporus mushrooms during refrigerated storage. Molecular dynamics simulations revealed favorable interactions between eugenol and silica that restrict mobility, enabling sustained release. The system showed medium-dependent release kinetics following the Weibull model, offering a potential solution for extending mushroom shelf life.

papers.title: CFHR5 promotes colorectal cancer by upregulating COX4I2 to inhibit ferroptosis.
papers.abstract: Colorectal cancer (CRC) remains one of the most common gastrointestinal malignancies worldwide, characterized by high incidence and mortality rates. Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death, has emerged as a critical mechanism in tumor biology. However, the regulatory role of CFHR5 (Complement Factor H-Related Protein 5) in ferroptosis and CRC progression remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study identifies CFHR5 as a promoter of colorectal cancer by upregulating COX4I2, which inhibits ferroptosis, a form of regulated cell death. The findings suggest that targeting CFHR5 could be a potential therapeutic strategy for colorectal cancer.

papers.title: Collective fluctuations underlying nanobody inhibitory activity targeting B. anthracis S-layers revealed by multiscale simulations.
papers.abstract: Nanobodies (Nbs) that depolymerize bacterial surface-layers (S-layers) offer a route to antivirulence therapeutics, but their mechanisms have been difficult to infer from binding structures alone. In B. anthracis, several Nbs bind to Sap, the S-layer protein that assembles into a paracrystalline lattice surrounding the cell. Despite similar binding poses and sequences, only a subset of these ("inhibitory") Nbs induce disassembly of the protein lattice, ultimately abrogating pathogenicity. In this study, we leveraged multiscale simulations to test whether representative Nb-induced fluctuations
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study uses multiscale simulations to investigate why only some nanobodies (Nbs) that bind to the B. anthracis S-layer protein Sap are able to disassemble the protective protein lattice and inhibit pathogenicity. The simulations reveal that inhibitory Nbs induce specific collective fluctuations in the Sap lattice that are absent with non-inhibitory Nbs, providing a mechanistic explanation for their differential activity. This work demonstrates how computational approaches can uncover dynamic mechanisms of antibody-based therapeutics that are not apparent from static binding structures.

papers.title: Targeting bromodomain and extraterminal proteins in cardiovascular disease: Pathological mechanisms and therapeutic applications.
papers.abstract: Bromodomain and extraterminal proteins have emerged as key epigenetic regulators that coordinate transcriptional programs fundamental to cardiovascular physiology and disease. Although bromodomain and extraterminal protein inhibitors exert multiple cardiovascular effects, they cannot fully explain clinical heterogeneity in outcomes and responses, highlighting the need to clarify the context- and disease-specific roles of individual bromodomain and extraterminal protein family members. This narrative review delineates the distinct molecular roles of bromodomain-containing protein 2, bromodomain
papers.journal: The Journal of international medical research
papers.summary: This review examines how bromodomain and extraterminal (BET) proteins regulate gene expression in cardiovascular health and disease, and discusses the potential of BET inhibitors as therapies. The authors highlight that current inhibitors have variable clinical effects, emphasizing the need to understand the specific roles of individual BET family members like BRD2.

papers.title: OrgNet+: towards robust protein stability prediction with convolutional neural networks.
papers.abstract: Predicting the effect of single-point mutations on protein stability is a central problem in molecular biology and protein engineering. Recent structure-based deep learning methods, particularly 3D convolutional neural networks (3D CNNs), have achieved strong predictive performance by leveraging high-resolution protein structures. However, proteins exist as heterogeneous conformational ensembles rather than single static structures, and the impact of conformational flexibility on structure-based ΔΔG predictors remains poorly characterized. Consequently, current models may yield unstable or eve
papers.summary: This study introduces OrgNet+, a 3D convolutional neural network that improves protein stability prediction by accounting for conformational flexibility, addressing a key limitation of existing structure-based methods that rely on single static structures. The authors demonstrate that incorporating multiple conformations leads to more robust predictions of mutation effects on protein stability.

papers.title: seq2ribo: structure-aware integration of machine learning and simulation to predict ribosome location profiles from RNA sequences.
papers.abstract: Ribosome dynamics are vital in the process of protein expression. Current methods rely on ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq), RNA-seq profiles, and full genomic context. This restricts their use in de novo sequence design, like messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccines. Simulation-only approaches like the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process (TASEP) oversimplify translation by focusing solely on codon elongation times.
papers.summary: This paper presents seq2ribo, a machine learning method that predicts ribosome location profiles directly from RNA sequences without requiring experimental data like Ribo-seq or full genomic context. By integrating TASEP simulations with a neural network, it enables de novo prediction for applications such as mRNA vaccine design.

papers.title: Computational and experimental evidence supports the modulation of inflammaging-related pathways by Sorghum bicolor 3-deoxyanthocyanidins.
papers.abstract: Chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and metabolic dysfunction are central drivers of biological aging. Polyphenolics from Sorghum bicolor, particularly 3-deoxyanthocyanidins, have the potential to modulate biological aging.
papers.summary: This study provides computational and experimental evidence that 3-deoxyanthocyanidins from Sorghum bicolor can modulate pathways related to inflammaging, a chronic inflammation-driven process of biological aging. The findings suggest these polyphenolics may counteract oxidative stress and metabolic dysfunction associated with aging.

papers.title: Molecular dynamics and predictive toxicity insights into theaflavin as a potential inhibitor of MMP-2 and MMP-9 in breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Breast cancer is one of the most commonly diagnosed malignancies in women and remains a leading cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide. Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), particularly Matrix Metalloproteinase-2 (MMP-2) and Matrix Metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9), play critical roles in cancer invasion, metastasis, and angiogenesis, making them attractive therapeutic targets. Theaflavin, a major polyphenolic compound derived from black tea (Camellia sinensis), has demonstrated anticancer properties, notably through modulation of the nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB) signaling pathway and suppressio
papers.summary: This study uses computational modeling to investigate theaflavin, a compound from black tea, as a potential inhibitor of MMP-2 and MMP-9, which are key proteins involved in breast cancer spread. Molecular dynamics simulations and toxicity predictions suggest theaflavin may bind stably to these targets with favorable safety profiles, supporting its potential as a natural therapeutic agent.

papers.title: Chemical multiplexing in the nervous system: molecular architecture, functional stratification, and pathophysiological plasticity of neuropeptide-classical neurotransmitter cotransmission.
papers.abstract: Neurons frequently synthesize both neuropeptides and classical low-molecular-weight neurotransmitters, challenging the historical interpretation of one-neuron-one-transmitter signaling. Rather than representing biochemical redundancy, coexistence reflects a conserved organizational strategy that expands neural coding capacity through chemical multiplexing. Dual vesicular architecture-small clear vesicles for rapid synaptic transmission and large dense-core vesicles for activity-dependent peptide release-creates stimulus-intensity-dependent recruitment of distinct signaling layers. Classical tr
papers.summary: This review challenges the historical 'one-neuron-one-transmitter' dogma by presenting evidence that neurons commonly co-release neuropeptides and classical neurotransmitters, a phenomenon termed 'chemical multiplexing.' The authors describe a dual vesicular architecture where small clear vesicles mediate rapid synaptic transmission while large dense-core vesicles enable activity-dependent peptide release, expanding neural coding capacity. They further discuss how this multiplexing system shows pathophysiological plasticity, with implications for understanding neurological and psychiatric disorders.

papers.title: Computational evaluation of selected compounds as potential inhibitors of gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase using molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, MMGBSA, and ADMET analyses.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the inhibitory potential of selected phytochemicals and conventional drugs against gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, an important therapeutic target associated with acid-related gastrointestinal disorders such as peptic ulcers and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Computational approaches, including molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, MMGBSA free energy calculations, and ADMET profiling, were employed to evaluate the pharmacological relevance of the selected compounds. Molecular docking analysis revealed that several compounds exhibited favorable interactions with the
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to evaluate how well certain plant compounds and existing drugs might inhibit gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, a key target for treating acid-related stomach disorders. The analysis identified several compounds with promising binding interactions, suggesting they could be further explored as potential treatments for conditions like peptic ulcers and GERD.

papers.title: Computational QSAR and structure-based identification of plerixafor-derived PIM-1 kinase inhibitors in diffuse large B-Cell lymphoma.
papers.abstract: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is the most common and aggressive subtype of non-Hodgkin lymphoma, with a substantial proportion of patients developing resistance to standard chemotherapy. Chromosomal translocations resulting in overexpression of the serine/threonine kinase Proviral Integration site for Moloney murine leukemia virus (PIM-1) contribute to disease progression, therapeutic resistance, and poor clinical outcomes, establishing PIM-1 as a promising molecular target. Unlike many kinases, the ATP-binding site of PIM-1 lacks a backbone hydrogen bond donor within the hinge region
papers.summary: This study used computational QSAR and structure-based methods to identify plerixafor-derived compounds as potential PIM-1 kinase inhibitors for treating diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. The work targets PIM-1, a kinase overexpressed in chemotherapy-resistant DLBCL, by exploiting its unique ATP-binding site lacking a hinge region hydrogen bond donor. The findings offer a computational approach to repurpose plerixafor derivatives for targeted cancer therapy.

papers.title: Structural homology of HHV-6B epitopes as candidates for molecular mimicry triggers of the onset type one diabetes mellitus.
papers.abstract: To study whether HHV-6B can act as a molecular mimic to trigger onset of Type One Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) by assessing structural and binding similarities between HHV-6B derived epitopes and T1DM autoantigen derived epitopes.
papers.summary: This study investigates whether human herpesvirus 6B (HHV-6B) might trigger type 1 diabetes through molecular mimicry, finding structural and binding similarities between viral epitopes and diabetes-related autoantigens. The results suggest a potential mechanism where HHV-6B infection could initiate an autoimmune attack on pancreatic cells.

papers.title: Rational design 2.0: transitioning from static structural biology to computational prioritization and iterative vaccine optimization for RSV.
papers.abstract: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a major cause of severe lower respiratory tract disease (LRTD) in infants and older adults worldwide. Although vaccines based on the fusion (F) protein have shown progress, their efficacy remains limited by antigenic instability and viral evolution. The metastable transition of the F protein between prefusion (preF) and postF conformations critically determines the exposure of neutralizing epitopes, with most potent antibodies targeting preF-specific sites. In addition, the glycosylated G protein contributes to immune evasion through glycan shielding and CX
papers.summary: This paper reviews the challenges in developing effective RSV vaccines due to the metastable nature of the fusion (F) protein and immune evasion by the G protein, and proposes a transition from static structural biology to computational prioritization and iterative vaccine optimization. The authors argue that rational design 2.0, integrating computational methods, can overcome antigenic instability and viral evolution to improve vaccine efficacy.

papers.title: Convergent GenX biodegradation by genomically designed and functionally screened synthetic bacterial consortia.
papers.abstract: GenX (hexafluoropropylene oxide dimer acid) has been introduced as a replacement for legacy polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) compounds; however, accumulating evidence indicates this C6 ether-PFAS may exhibit comparable toxicity to PFOA while persisting in contaminated water supplies. Biological remediation offers genuine mineralization potential but has been constrained by the exceptional stability of carbon-fluorine bonds. Here, we demonstrate that synthetic bacterial consortia assembled through rational genomic design (GENIA) or microfluidic selection (Community G) achieve rapid GenX biode
papers.summary: Researchers developed two approaches—rational genomic design and microfluidic functional screening—to assemble synthetic bacterial consortia that can rapidly biodegrade GenX, a replacement PFAS compound with potential toxicity similar to PFOA. This work demonstrates that engineered microbial communities can overcome the stability of carbon-fluorine bonds, offering a promising biological remediation strategy for persistent water contaminants.

papers.title: Liposomal Nanoparticulate Drug Delivery Systems: Strategies to Destabilize Biological Membranes at the Target Tissue.
papers.abstract: Liposomal nanoparticulate drug delivery systems (LNDDSs) are clinically validated nanomedicine platforms seeing regular use in oncology and infectious disease. Their applications have rapidly expanded with several tissue targeting formulations in early-phase clinical trials. Beyond small molecular drugs, LNDDSs are increasingly employed for delivery of nucleic acid therapeutics, such as ribonucleic acid (RNA) based vaccines and immunomodulators. Recent advances in nanomaterials have enabled LNDDSs not only to transport therapeutic agents across systemic biological barriers but also to selectiv
papers.summary: This review discusses liposomal nanoparticulate drug delivery systems (LNDDSs), which are clinically validated platforms used in oncology and infectious disease. It highlights their expanding applications, including targeted formulations in clinical trials and delivery of nucleic acid therapeutics like RNA vaccines, enabled by recent nanomaterial advances.

papers.title: Design, Synthesis and Biological Evaluation of New Oxazolidinone Antimicrobial Agents with Low Monoamine Oxidase A Inhibition.
papers.abstract: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial agent that acts by binding to the 23S rRNA of the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit to inhibit protein synthesis and exhibits potent activity against Gram-positive pathogens and Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Although resistance to linezolid remains relatively low, its clinical use is limited by safety concerns arising from inhibition of monoamine oxidase A (MAO-A). To address this limitation, we designed and synthesized a series of novel oxazolidinone derivatives with reduced MAO-A inhibition and preserved antibacterial potency.
papers.summary: Researchers designed new oxazolidinone antibiotics that retain antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria and Mycobacterium tuberculosis while reducing the safety risk of monoamine oxidase A inhibition, a side effect that limits the clinical use of the existing drug linezolid.
